confidence.disclosure: Omar Khayyam (Ghiyath al-Din Abu'l-Fath Umar ibn Ibrahim al-Nishapuri al-Khayyami) is well documented as a scientist by his contemporaries Nizami Aruzi (Chahar Maqala) and al-Bayhaqi (Tatimmat Siwan al-Hikma), and his major scientific commissions are firmly placed: the calendar reform under Malik Shah and his work at the Isfahan observatory. His birth year of 1048 (which he himself implicitly gives in an astrological note) and death in Nishapur in 1131 are firm. The famous quatrains (rubaiyat) attributed to him are mostly of doubtful authenticity, accumulated over centuries.

sources: Edward FitzGerald, introduction to The Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam (translation, 1859)
Mehdi Aminrazavi, The Wine of Wisdom (2005)

journey.event: Born on 18 May 1048 in Nishapur in Khurasan (modern northeastern Iran), one of the four great cities of Khurasan and a major centre of trade and learning. The exact date is reconstructed from a horoscope cast by his contemporary al-Bayhaqi. His name al-Khayyami (the tent-maker) suggests his father's trade.

Studies at the great madrasa of Nishapur, in the company of (according to a famous later legend) Hasan Sabbah and Nizam al-Mulk - though this story is chronologically impossible since Nizam al-Mulk was already an adult. His real teachers in Nishapur include Imam Muwaffaq al-Din.

Around the age of 20 travels to Samarkand in Transoxiana, where the Karakhanid governor Abu Tahir takes him under his patronage. Composes there his Treatise on Demonstrations of Problems of Algebra (Risala fi al-Barahin 'ala Masa'il al-Jabr wa'l-Muqabala), the most important Arabic algebra after al-Khwarizmi.

In his algebra treatise gives a complete classification of cubic equations and provides geometric solutions to all of them by means of intersecting conic sections - the most advanced work in algebra anywhere in the world at that time. Latin Europe will not match this achievement until the 16th century.
In 1074 is invited by the Seljuk sultan Malik Shah and his vizier Nizam al-Mulk to head a new astronomical observatory in Isfahan, the Seljuk capital. Travels south to Isfahan and takes up his post.

Heads a team of leading astronomers at the Isfahan observatory. They prepare astronomical tables (the Zij-i Malikshahi, lost) and undertake a major calendar reform. Khayyam's calculation of the length of the solar year (365.24219858 days) is more accurate than the Gregorian calendar that Europe will adopt 500 years later.

On 15 March 1079, Khayyam's reformed calendar - the Jalali calendar - takes effect by decree of Malik Shah. It is named after the sultan's regnal title Jalal al-Din. With minor modifications, this is still the official calendar of Iran and Afghanistan today.

Continues his work at Isfahan in mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy. Writes commentaries on the parallel postulate of Euclid, anticipating non-Euclidean geometry by centuries. Also composes philosophical treatises in the tradition of Avicenna.

In 1092 his patron Nizam al-Mulk is assassinated (allegedly by an Assassin sent by Hasan Sabbah) and Malik Shah dies a few weeks later. The Isfahan observatory loses its funding and effectively closes. Khayyam's settled court life is over.
Around 1095 makes the pilgrimage to Mecca, partly (according to some sources) to clear himself of the suspicions of impiety that have followed him at court because of his openly philosophical views.

Around 1100 settles for a time in Merv (in modern Turkmenistan), then the capital of the Seljuk sultan Sanjar, son of Malik Shah. Continues to work as court astronomer and adviser, but is increasingly frustrated by the political climate.

Around 1112 Khayyam is in Balkh (in modern Afghanistan), where the writer Nizami Aruzi meets him in person and records that he predicted his own burial place would lie 'where the trees twice each year shall scatter blossoms over my tomb' - a prophecy Nizami later confirmed when he visited Khayyam's grave in Nishapur.

Dies in Nishapur, his birthplace, around 1131 (526 AH), aged about 83. Tradition (preserved by Nizami Aruzi) says he died reading Avicenna's Kitab al-Shifa, kissed the book, made a will, and passed away. He is buried in Nishapur, near the shrine of Imamzadeh Mahruq; the present mausoleum, built in 1963, is one of the most visited monuments in Iran.
